statements.text: Highest gas flow occur at the end of expiration
statements.explanation: Flow is highest at the very beginning of forced expiration when lung volume is largest, and it drops to zero at the end. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: Alveolar ventilation

statements.text: Is increased by a reduction in arterial PO2
statements.explanation: Low blood oxygen triggers the peripheral chemoreceptors to send signals that rapidly increase breathing rate and depth. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Becomes zero when anatomical dead space equals tidal volume
statements.explanation: If you take a shallow breath that is entirely stuck in the windpipe (dead space), zero fresh air actually reaches the alveoli. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Would increase by increasing impulse discharge from the respiratory centers
statements.explanation: More nerve impulses from the brainstem command the diaphragm to work faster and harder, drawing in more air. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Is relatively lower in males than females
statements.explanation: Males generally have larger lung capacities and tidal volumes due to body size, making absolute alveolar ventilation higher. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Is influenced by individual's activity
statements.explanation: Exercise vastly increases oxygen demand and CO2 production, which directly drives up alveolar ventilation to compensate. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: The carotid bodies

statements.text: Are stretch receptors in the walls of internal carotid arteries
statements.explanation: The carotid *bodies* are chemoreceptors. The stretch receptors (baroreceptors) are located in the nearby carotid *sinus*. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Have a blood flow per unit weight similar to the brain
statements.explanation: They have the highest blood flow per gram of tissue of any organ in the body, far exceeding the brain, to sample arterial blood instantly. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Are influenced more by blood PO2 than by oxygen content
statements.explanation: They sense the dissolved partial pressure of oxygen (PO2). Anemic patients have low oxygen content but normal PO2, so the bodies do not fire. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Generate more afferent impulses when blood H+ concentration rises
statements.explanation: High acid levels (H+) directly stimulate these receptors to increase firing, forcing the lungs to blow off acidic CO2. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: And the aortic bodies are mainly responsible for the increase in ventilation in hypoxia
statements.explanation: The central brain chemoreceptors do not sense low oxygen. Only these peripheral bodies sound the alarm for hypoxia. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: A rise in the arterial PCO2 leads to an increase in

statements.text: Ventilation due to stimulation of peripheral chemoreceptors
statements.explanation: While they primarily sense oxygen, peripheral chemoreceptors also react quickly to high CO2, aiding the breathing drive. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Arterial blood pressure
statements.explanation: High CO2 strongly stimulates the brain's vasomotor center, creating massive sympathetic outflow that spikes blood pressure. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Cerebral blood pressure
statements.explanation: CO2 is the most potent dilator of brain blood vessels. Dilating them floods the brain with blood, raising local pressure and flow. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Ventilation due to stimulation of central chemoreceptors
statements.explanation: This is the primary and most powerful control mechanism; CO2 crosses into the brain fluid, forms acid, and commands heavy breathing. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: The plasma bicarbonate levels
statements.explanation: CO2 reacts with water in the blood to create carbonic acid, which dissociates slightly, creating a measurable rise in bicarbonate ions. [Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition]

questions.stem: As blood passes through the systemic capillaries:

statements.text: pH rises
statements.explanation: Tissues dump acidic CO2 into the blood, making the blood more acidic, which means the pH falls, not rises. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: HCO3- ions pass from the red blood cells to plasma
statements.explanation: CO2 enters the red cell, turns into bicarbonate, and is pumped out into the plasma in exchange for chloride (the chloride shift). [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Cl- ions concentration in the red blood cells falls
statements.explanation: Because bicarbonate leaves the cell, chloride rushes IN to balance the electrical charge (chloride shift). So Cl- concentration rises. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Its oxygen dissociation curve shifts to the right
statements.explanation: The acidic CO2 entering the blood weakens hemoglobin's grip on oxygen (the Bohr effect), shifting the curve right to unload oxygen. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Its ability to deliver oxygen to tissues is enhanced
statements.explanation: The rightward shift of the curve (Bohr effect) is exactly what allows hemoglobin to easily dump its oxygen into the needy tissues. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: With respect to oxygen - haemoglobin dissociation/association:

statements.text: A high P50 may represent an increased carbon dioxide uptake from tissue
statements.explanation: A high P50 means a rightward shift. High CO2 in tissues causes this shift (Bohr effect), forcing oxygen off and taking CO2 on. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: A rise in 2:3 DPG in RBCs increases oxygen off-loading
statements.explanation: 2,3-DPG wedges itself into hemoglobin, forcing it into a shape that hates oxygen, causing it to dump oxygen into tissues. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: A fall in temperature would increase haemoglobin affinity to oxygen
statements.explanation: Cold temperatures make hemoglobin hold onto oxygen very tightly (left shift), which is why cold tissues can become hypoxic. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: A rise in pH increases haemoglobin affinity for oxygen
statements.explanation: A high pH (alkalosis) means low acid. Without acid forcing oxygen off, hemoglobin holds onto it tightly (left shift). [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: A fall in the alveolar PO2 to 80 mm Hg would greatly affect Hb saturation
statements.explanation: The top of the oxygen curve is very flat. A drop from 100 to 80 mmHg barely changes saturation (it stays above 95%). [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: The following are features of acclimatization to high altitude:

statements.text: Increased levels of erythropoietin
statements.explanation: Low oxygen stimulates the kidneys to release EPO, which builds more red blood cells to carry oxygen in the thin air. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Hyperventilation
statements.explanation: The peripheral chemoreceptors sense the low oxygen and immediately force you to breathe faster and deeper. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Increased plasma 2-3 DPG
statements.explanation: The red blood cells produce more 2,3-DPG, which shifts the oxygen curve right, helping deliver what little oxygen there is to the tissues. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Increased mitochondria density
statements.explanation: Cells adapt to chronic low oxygen by building more energy factories (mitochondria) to use oxygen more efficiently. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Pulmonary vasoconstriction
statements.explanation: Hypoxic air in the lungs causes lung blood vessels to tightly constrict. At high altitude, this happens everywhere, causing pulmonary hypertension. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: Transport of:

statements.text: CO2 in blood is associated with a shift of chloride ions from RBC into plasma
statements.explanation: It is the opposite; bicarbonate leaves the RBC into the plasma, and chloride shifts FROM the plasma INTO the RBC to replace it. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: O2 in physical state is about 15 ml O2 /dl of blood
statements.explanation: Only about 0.3 ml of oxygen is physically dissolved in a deciliter of plasma. Hemoglobin carries the massive 15-20 ml load. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: O2 requires combination with iron atom in haemoglobin
statements.explanation: Oxygen binds reversibly to the central iron atom (in the Fe2+ state) located inside each heme group of the hemoglobin molecule. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: CO2 is mainly as bicarbonate
statements.explanation: About 70% of all carbon dioxide transported in the blood is carried in the form of dissolved bicarbonate ions (HCO3-). [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: CO2 carbamino compound may occur in plasma
statements.explanation: CO2 can bind to the amino ends of plasma proteins (like albumin), forming small amounts of carbamino compounds in the plasma itself. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: The Peripheral chemoreceptor responses to:

statements.text: Raised body temperature results in a decrease in the breathing frequency
statements.explanation: A fever increases the body's metabolic demand, which triggers an increase in breathing rate (panting/tachypnea), not a decrease. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Raised blood pH is associated with hyperventilation
statements.explanation: Raised pH (alkalosis) tells the brain there is too little acid. The body responds by hypoventilating (breathing less) to hold onto acidic CO2. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Reduced blood perfusion results in reduced heart rate
statements.explanation: Poor blood flow causes local hypoxia in the chemoreceptors, which triggers a massive sympathetic response that speeds up, not slows, the heart. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Raised PaCO2 increases the depth of breathing
statements.explanation: High carbon dioxide is the strongest trigger to breathe; it forces deep, rapid breaths to blow the toxic gas out. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Lowered PaO2 would result in hypoventilation
statements.explanation: Low oxygen causes a panic response from the peripheral chemoreceptors, resulting in hyperventilation to get more air in. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

questions.stem: Shunted blood in pulmonary capillaries:

statements.text: Have PO2 of 100mm Hg
statements.explanation: Shunted blood skips the alveoli and never picks up oxygen, so its PO2 stays at the low venous level of about 40 mmHg. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Have PCO2 less than 45mm Hg
statements.explanation: Since it doesn't get to exhale its CO2, the blood keeps its high venous PCO2 level, which is usually 45-46 mmHg. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Normal oxygen saturation
statements.explanation: Because it didn't get oxygenated, its saturation stays at the deoxygenated venous level, which is roughly 75%. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Have PO2 of 40mm Hg
statements.explanation: Because it is essentially just venous blood that bypassed the lungs without touching fresh air, it retains the venous PO2 of 40 mmHg. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

statements.text: Have PCO2 of 40 mmHg
statements.explanation: A PCO2 of 40 mmHg is normal *arterial* blood. Shunted venous blood has a higher PCO2, around 45-46 mmHg. [Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition]

definitions.Atropine: A medication that acts as a competitive antagonist for muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, blocking the parasympathetic nervous system.
definitions.Choleretics: Substances that stimulate the liver to increase the production and secretion of bile.
definitions.Steatorrhea: The excretion of abnormal quantities of fat with the feces owing to reduced absorption of fat by the intestine.
definitions.Oxyntic cells: Also known as parietal cells, these are the epithelial cells in the stomach that secrete hydrochloric acid (HCl) and intrinsic factor.
definitions.Vagotomy: A surgical operation in which one or more branches of the vagus nerve are cut, typically to reduce the rate of gastric secretion.
definitions.Aquaporin-1 (AQP1): A water channel protein that facilitates the rapid transport of water across cell membranes, abundantly found in the proximal tubule and descending limb of the loop of Henle.
definitions.Pneumotaxic center: A neural center in the upper pons of the brainstem that helps regulate the rate and depth of respiration by sending inhibitory signals to the inspiratory center.
definitions.2,3-DPG: 2,3-Diphosphoglycerate is a molecule in red blood cells that binds to hemoglobin and decreases its affinity for oxygen, promoting oxygen release to tissues.

questions.stem: Salivary secretion:

statements.text: Is entirely under neural control.
statements.explanation: Unlike most of the GI tract, the salivary glands are not primarily regulated by hormones; their secretion is controlled almost exclusively by the autonomic nervous system. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Has a constant composition regardless of the rate of secretion.
statements.explanation: As the rate of saliva flow increases, the salivary ducts have less time to reabsorb sodium and chloride, causing the ionic composition to change and become closer to plasma. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Is increased after injection of atropine.
statements.explanation: Atropine blocks the parasympathetic nervous system, which severely inhibits salivary secretion and causes dry mouth. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: is markedly increased in amount after sympathetic stimulation.
statements.explanation: Sympathetic stimulation typically produces a very small volume of thick, viscous saliva, whereas parasympathetic stimulation produces a large volume of watery saliva. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: By the parotid gland is about 70% of the total secretion
statements.explanation: The submandibular glands, not the parotid glands, produce the majority (about 70%) of the total resting salivary secretion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Constituents of saliva include:

statements.text: Ammonia
statements.explanation: Saliva contains various metabolic waste products in small amounts, including ammonia and urea. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: HCL
statements.explanation: Hydrochloric acid (HCl) is secreted by the stomach, not the salivary glands; saliva is typically slightly alkaline or neutral to protect teeth. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Prostaglandins
statements.explanation: While present in trace amounts in many bodily fluids, prostaglandins are not considered a primary functional constituent of saliva for digestion. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Immunoglobulins
statements.explanation: Saliva contains Immunoglobulin A (IgA), which helps destroy bacteria and protects the oral cavity from infection. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Calcium
statements.explanation: Saliva contains calcium and phosphate, which are essential for preventing the dissolution of tooth enamel and promoting remineralization. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: With regard to electrolyte present in the saliva

statements.text: Sodium decreases when the rate of saliva secretion increases
statements.explanation: When the secretion rate is high, ductal cells have less time to reabsorb sodium, so the concentration of sodium in the saliva actually increases. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Chloride ion does not change with the rate of secretion
statements.explanation: Chloride mirrors sodium; at higher flow rates, less chloride is reabsorbed by the ducts, leading to a higher chloride concentration in final saliva. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Calcium is present in saliva at a higher concentration than the plasma
statements.explanation: The concentration of free ionized calcium in saliva is generally lower than that found in blood plasma. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Magnesium is present at lower concentration than the plasma
statements.explanation: Like calcium, magnesium is maintained at lower concentrations in the saliva compared to the blood plasma. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Potassium level decreases during ductal modification of saliva
statements.explanation: The salivary ducts actively secrete potassium into the saliva; therefore, ductal modification increases the potassium level relative to plasma. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

questions.stem: The appetite for food is lost when

statements.text: Satiety center in the hypothalamus is stimulated
statements.explanation: Stimulation of the ventromedial nuclei of the hypothalamus (the satiety center) induces a feeling of fullness and suppresses the desire for food. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Appetite center in the hypothalamus is destroyed
statements.explanation: Destruction of the lateral hypothalamic area (the feeding center) eliminates the drive to eat, leading to severe anorexia. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: The stomach is distended
statements.explanation: Stretch receptors in the stomach wall send inhibitory signals via the vagus nerve to the feeding center, reducing appetite. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: The stomach is surgically removed
statements.explanation: While ghrelin (a hunger hormone) levels drop after a gastrectomy, appetite is regulated by many other factors (like glucose, leptin, and intestinal hormones) and is not completely lost. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Blood glucose level falls
statements.explanation: A drop in blood glucose (hypoglycemia) activates the hunger center, powerfully stimulating the appetite rather than causing its loss. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Swallowing is accompanied by the following:

statements.text: Relaxation of the upper esophageal sphincter.
statements.explanation: The upper esophageal sphincter relaxes to allow the food bolus to pass from the pharynx into the esophagus. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Contraction of the pharyngeal wall muscles.
statements.explanation: The superior, middle, and inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscles contract in sequence to push the food downward. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Opening of the larynx by epiglottis.
statements.explanation: During swallowing, the epiglottis swings backward to cover and firmly close the opening of the larynx, preventing aspiration into the airway. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Pulling the soft palate downwards.
statements.explanation: The soft palate is pulled upward to close off the posterior nares, preventing food from entering the nasal cavity. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Inhibition of respiration
statements.explanation: The swallowing center in the medulla briefly halts the respiratory center (deglutition apnea) so that breathing stops during the swallow. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Gastric secretion is increased by:

statements.text: Sympathetic stimulation.
statements.explanation: Sympathetic nervous system stimulation generally decreases overall gastrointestinal motility and secretion, including gastric acid. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Acid in the duodenum.
statements.explanation: Acid entering the duodenum stimulates the release of secretin, which inhibits gastric acid secretion and stomach emptying. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Stimulation of stretch receptors in the wall of the stomach.
statements.explanation: Stretching the stomach wall activates local enteric reflexes and long vagovagal reflexes that stimulate gastric juice secretion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Hypertonicity of duodenum.
statements.explanation: Hypertonic fluids in the duodenum trigger enterogastric reflexes that inhibit gastric secretion and slow down stomach emptying. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Secretin hormone
statements.explanation: Secretin, released by the duodenum in response to low pH, acts to inhibit gastric acid secretion from the stomach parietal cells. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following can cause delayed gastric emptying:

statements.text: Vagotomy
statements.explanation: The vagus nerve is responsible for coordinating the strong stomach contractions needed for emptying; cutting it (vagotomy) severely delays gastric emptying. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Fat in the duodenum
statements.explanation: Fats trigger the release of Cholecystokinin (CCK), which strongly inhibits gastric emptying to allow time for fat digestion. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Secretin
statements.explanation: Secretin responds to acid in the duodenum and acts to decrease stomach motility and delay gastric emptying. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Increased fluidity of the chyme
statements.explanation: Liquids and highly fluid chyme empty from the stomach much faster than solid or highly viscous meals. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Gastric inhibitory peptide
statements.explanation: Gastric Inhibitory Peptide (GIP), released in response to fats and carbohydrates in the duodenum, mildly inhibits gastric motility and delays emptying. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Concerning HCl secretion:

statements.text: It is associated with increased pH of the blood
statements.explanation: As parietal cells secrete H+ into the stomach lumen, they simultaneously transport bicarbonate into the blood, leading to a temporary increase in blood pH known as the 'alkaline tide'. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: It is stimulated by norepinephrine.
statements.explanation: Norepinephrine is a sympathetic neurotransmitter that generally inhibits digestive secretions, whereas acetylcholine (parasympathetic) stimulates HCl secretion. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: The energy required is derived from ATP breakdown.
statements.explanation: The secretion of hydrogen ions is driven against a massive concentration gradient by the H+/K+ ATPase pump, which heavily consumes ATP. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: There is an active transport of H+ from the oxyntic cells into the gastric lumen and K+ in the opposite direction.
statements.explanation: The proton pump on the luminal membrane exchanges intracellular H+ for luminal K+ using primary active transport. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: H+ results from dissociation of water
statements.explanation: Inside the parietal cell, water dissociates into H+ and OH-. The H+ is pumped into the lumen, and OH- combines with CO2 to form bicarbonate. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following may lead to an increased gastric acid secretion

statements.text: Increased D cell activity
statements.explanation: D cells secrete somatostatin, which exerts a strong inhibitory effect on parietal cells and reduces gastric acid secretion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Increased G cell activity
statements.explanation: G cells secrete gastrin, a hormone that travels through the blood to powerfully stimulate the parietal cells to secrete more acid. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Increased vagal discharge
statements.explanation: Vagal stimulation releases acetylcholine directly onto parietal cells and stimulates G cells, resulting in a large increase in acid secretion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: H. pylori infection
statements.explanation: H. pylori can inflame the gastric mucosa, suppress inhibitory D cells, and hyperstimulate G cells, leading to excess acid production. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Fresh Milk uptake
statements.explanation: Although milk temporarily buffers acid, its high protein and calcium content strongly stimulate gastrin release, causing a rebound increase in gastric acid. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Gastric mucosal barrier is maintained by the following:

statements.text: The nature of surface membrane of the gastric mucosal cells and the tight junctions between these cells
statements.explanation: Tight junctions physically lock the epithelial cells together, creating an impermeable barrier that prevents acid from leaking into underlying tissue. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Certain prostaglandins
statements.explanation: Prostaglandins (like PGE2) are crucial for maintaining blood flow to the mucosa and stimulating the production of protective mucus and bicarbonate. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: The mucous gel-like membrane that coats the gastric mucous membrane
statements.explanation: Surface mucous cells secrete a thick, alkaline gel that blankets the stomach lining, acting as a physical shield against pepsin and acid. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Excessive amount of bile salts
statements.explanation: Bile salts refluxed into the stomach act as detergents that strip away the lipid layer of the mucosa, severely disrupting and breaking the barrier. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Decreased blood flow to the GIT
statements.explanation: Ischemia (decreased blood flow) deprives the mucosa of the oxygen and nutrients needed to produce mucus and rapidly turnover cells, leading to barrier breakdown and ulcers. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Concerning pancreatic juice:

statements.text: Gastrin is an important stimulant for secretion of bicarbonate by the pancreas.
statements.explanation: The hormone secretin, not gastrin, is the primary stimulant that causes the pancreatic ducts to secrete a massive volume of bicarbonate-rich fluid. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Somatostatin inhibits secretion of pancreatic juice.
statements.explanation: Somatostatin is a universal inhibitory hormone in the GI tract that acts to reduce both endocrine and exocrine pancreatic secretions. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Contains enzymes for digestion of carbohydrate.
statements.explanation: Pancreatic juice contains copious amounts of pancreatic alpha-amylase, which digests starches into smaller sugars. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Sight or smell of food does not affect pancreatic secretion.
statements.explanation: The cephalic phase of digestion, triggered by the sight or smell of food, stimulates pancreatic enzyme secretion via the vagus nerve. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Vagotomy will cause decreased secretion
statements.explanation: Because the vagus nerve provides significant stimulatory input to the pancreas during the cephalic and gastric phases, cutting it decreases enzyme output. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Regarding the physiology of pancreas

statements.text: CCK stimulates secretion of pancreatic juice rich in bicarbonate
statements.explanation: Cholecystokinin (CCK) primarily stimulates the pancreatic acinar cells to secrete digestive enzymes, whereas secretin stimulates bicarbonate. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: CCK stimulates the contraction of gall bladder and relaxation of sphincter of Oddi
statements.explanation: CCK is the key physiological hormone that forces the gallbladder to empty bile into the intestine by contracting the bladder wall and relaxing the sphincter. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Trypsinogen is activated to trypsin by enterokinase
statements.explanation: The brush-border enzyme enterokinase (enteropeptidase) snips the inactive trypsinogen into active trypsin, which then kicks off a cascade activating all other pancreatic enzymes. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Enterokinase is a hormone secreted by pancreas
statements.explanation: Enterokinase is an enzyme, not a hormone, and it is located on the mucosal lining of the duodenum, not secreted by the pancreas. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Secretin stimulates secretion of pancreatic juice rich in enzymes
statements.explanation: Secretin stimulates the duct cells of the pancreas to secrete a watery fluid rich in bicarbonate, not enzymes. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following enzymes and their functions are correctly paired

statements.text: Chymotrypsin: hydrolyses cholesterol esters.
statements.explanation: Chymotrypsin is a protease that digests proteins into peptides. Cholesterol ester hydrolase is the enzyme that breaks down cholesterol esters. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Lactase: hydrolyses maltose to glucose molecule
statements.explanation: Lactase hydrolyzes lactose into glucose and galactose. Maltase is the enzyme responsible for hydrolyzing maltose into glucose. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: salivary alpha-amylase: hydrolyses starch to maltose
statements.explanation: Salivary amylase acts on dietary starch and breaks it down primarily into the disaccharide maltose. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Lipase: emulsifies fats.
statements.explanation: Lipase chemically digests fats by cleaving triglyceride bonds; it is the bile salts and lecithin that physically emulsify the fats into smaller droplets. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Deoxyribonuclease: hydrolyses protein to amino acids
statements.explanation: Deoxyribonuclease (DNase) breaks down DNA molecules. Proteases (like pepsin, trypsin, and peptidases) hydrolyze proteins into amino acids. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following substances must further be digested before they can be absorbed by specific carriers in intestinal cells

statements.text: Fructose
statements.explanation: Fructose is a monosaccharide and is fully digested; it is absorbed directly across the intestinal membrane by facilitated diffusion via GLUT5. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Sucrose
statements.explanation: Sucrose is a disaccharide that must be broken down by the brush-border enzyme sucrase into glucose and fructose before it can be absorbed. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Lactose
statements.explanation: Lactose is a disaccharide that must be split by lactase into glucose and galactose before absorption can occur. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Alanine
statements.explanation: Alanine is a single amino acid, the final product of protein digestion, and is absorbed directly by specific amino acid transporters. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Tripeptides
statements.explanation: Unlike larger carbohydrates, short peptide chains of two or three amino acids (tripeptides) do not need to be broken down to single amino acids; they are absorbed directly by the PepT1 transporter. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Regarding GIT

statements.text: Stomach contain content with highest pH
statements.explanation: Due to hydrochloric acid secretion, the stomach contains the most acidic environment in the body, meaning it has the lowest pH, not the highest. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Jejunum is the main site of Vitamin B12 absorption
statements.explanation: Vitamin B12, bound to intrinsic factor, bypasses the jejunum and is absorbed exclusively in the terminal ileum. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Colon is the site of action of aldosterone
statements.explanation: Similar to the kidney tubules, the epithelial cells of the colon respond to aldosterone by increasing sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Duodenum is the main site of iron absorption
statements.explanation: Most dietary iron is absorbed in the proximal small intestine, primarily the duodenum. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Large intestine is the site where major proportion of water is absorbed
statements.explanation: While the large intestine is crucial for final water desiccation to form feces, the small intestine absorbs the vast majority (about 8.5 of the 9 liters) of daily fluid. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Bile salts:

statements.text: Are derived from hemolyzed RBCs.
statements.explanation: Bile pigments (like bilirubin) are derived from hemolyzed RBCs, but bile salts are synthesized in the liver directly from cholesterol. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Facilitates breakdown of fats to fatty acids.
statements.explanation: Bile salts physically emulsify fats to increase surface area; it is the enzyme pancreatic lipase that chemically breaks down the fats into fatty acids. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Are among the most important physiologic choleretics.
statements.explanation: Recycled bile salts returning to the liver via the portal vein are the most powerful stimulants (choleretics) for the liver to secrete more bile. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Are actively absorbed at the terminal ileum.
statements.explanation: About 95% of bile salts are actively reabsorbed by specific transporters located in the terminal ileum to be recycled by the liver. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Deficiency may lead to rarefaction of bones
statements.explanation: Bile salt deficiency causes fat malabsorption, leading to poor absorption of fat-soluble vitamins, including Vitamin D, which can cause bone rarefaction (osteomalacia/rickets). (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Concerning absorption:

statements.text: Short-chain fatty acids are absorbed through lacteals.
statements.explanation: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids are water-soluble enough to enter the portal blood directly, unlike long-chain fatty acids which must be packaged into chylomicrons and enter the lacteals. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Bile salts are essential for digestion and absorption of carbohydrates.
statements.explanation: Bile salts are strictly necessary for the digestion and absorption of lipids (fats), not carbohydrates. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Steatorrhea means increased protein content in stool.
statements.explanation: Steatorrhea refers specifically to the presence of excess, unabsorbed fat in the stool, making it bulky and foul-smelling. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Steatorrhea occurs in obstructive jaundice.
statements.explanation: In obstructive jaundice, bile is blocked from reaching the intestine, preventing fat digestion and resulting in severe steatorrhea. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Vitamin B12 is absorbed at the terminal ileum
statements.explanation: The terminal ileum contains the specific receptors required to bind and absorb the Vitamin B12-intrinsic factor complex. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: In the colon:

statements.text: Bacterial synthesis of vitamin K is of vital importance.
statements.explanation: The normal colonic flora synthesizes significant amounts of Vitamin K, which is essential for blood clotting and provides a crucial supplement to dietary intake. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: More than half of the water that enters the colon is absorbed from the contents.
statements.explanation: The colon receives about 1.5 liters of chyme per day and absorbs almost all of it, leaving less than 100-200 ml of fluid in the feces. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Sympathetic stimulation results in enhanced motility.
statements.explanation: Sympathetic nervous system stimulation universally inhibits gastrointestinal motility, whereas parasympathetic stimulation enhances it. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: The secretion lacks significant digestive enzymes.
statements.explanation: The large intestine mucosa secretes alkaline mucus to lubricate feces and protect the lining, but it produces virtually no digestive enzymes. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Distension of distal colon initiate defecation
statements.explanation: As feces move into and stretch the walls of the rectum (distal colon), stretch receptors are triggered, initiating the defecation reflex. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: With respect to defecation reflex action

statements.text: It is coordinated by the reflex centers in the sacral cord
statements.explanation: The parasympathetic defecation reflex is integrated in the sacral segments of the spinal cord (S2-S4). (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: The afferent limb carries impulses from stretch receptors in the colon
statements.explanation: Afferent signals are generated when stretch receptors in the rectum and descending colon are distended by feces. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: The efferent limb travels mainly in sympathetic autonomic nerves
statements.explanation: The powerful efferent motor signals that drive defecation travel via parasympathetic fibers in the pelvic splanchnic nerves, not sympathetic nerves. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: it is more likely to be initiated just after a meal than just before it
statements.explanation: Filling the stomach triggers the gastrocolic and duodenocolic reflexes, which cause mass movements in the colon that often initiate defecation right after eating. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Can voluntarily be inhibited or facilitated
statements.explanation: Through conscious control of the external anal sphincter and the Valsalva maneuver, a person can either delay or aid the defecation reflex. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Liver functions include

statements.text: Haemopoiesis in adults
statements.explanation: While the liver is a primary site of blood cell formation (hemopoiesis) in the fetus, this function shifts entirely to the bone marrow in healthy adults. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Formation of lymph
statements.explanation: Because the hepatic sinusoids are highly permeable, the liver produces a massive amount of lymph, accounting for nearly half of all lymph formed in the body at rest. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Synthesis of bile
statements.explanation: Hepatocytes constantly synthesize bile, which is essential for the emulsification and absorption of dietary lipids. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Glycogenesis
statements.explanation: The liver plays a central role in glucose metabolism by storing excess blood glucose as glycogen (glycogenesis). (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Synthesis of immunoglobulin
statements.explanation: The liver synthesizes almost all plasma proteins (like albumin and clotting factors), but immunoglobulins (antibodies) are produced by plasma cells of the immune system. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following is correct regarding the thick ascending limb (TAL)

statements.text: There is passive absorption of salts
statements.explanation: Reabsorption of salts in the thick ascending limb is primarily an active process driven by the Na+/K+ ATPase pump and the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: There is high concentration of aquaporin 2 channels
statements.explanation: The thick ascending limb is virtually impermeable to water; aquaporin 2 channels are found exclusively in the late distal tubule and collecting ducts under ADH control. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: The filtrate has the highest concentration
statements.explanation: Because salts are actively removed while water remains trapped, the fluid in the thick ascending limb actually becomes highly diluted, not concentrated. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Luminal transport of Na is mainly through Na/K/2Cl channels (NKCC)
statements.explanation: Sodium enters the cells from the tubular lumen primarily by co-transport with potassium and chloride via the robust NKCC2 transporter. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Concentration gradient is created by Na+/H+ exchanger
statements.explanation: The medullary osmotic gradient is created by the action of the NKCC transporter in this segment, while the Na+/H+ exchanger is mostly active in the proximal tubule. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Regarding GFR

statements.text: Is about 180mls per day
statements.explanation: Normal Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) is roughly 125 ml per minute, which equals an enormous 180 LITERS per day, not milliliters. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Decreases when efferent arteriole constricts
statements.explanation: Mild to moderate constriction of the efferent arteriole actually increases glomerular hydrostatic pressure, thereby initially increasing GFR. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Is normally 20% of renal plasma flow
statements.explanation: The filtration fraction is the ratio of GFR to renal plasma flow, and it is normally about 0.20, meaning 20% of plasma is filtered. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: is maintained if mean arterial pressure is between 80 and 180mmHg
statements.explanation: Through intrinsic autoregulation mechanisms, the kidneys keep GFR remarkably constant despite wide fluctuations in blood pressure between 80 and 170-180 mmHg. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Increases when afferent arteriole dilates
statements.explanation: Dilation of the afferent arteriole allows more blood flow and higher hydrostatic pressure into the glomerulus, directly increasing GFR. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following is NOT involved in regulation of GFR

statements.text: Arterial pressure
statements.explanation: Arterial pressure is a primary determinant of GFR, even though it is buffered by autoregulation. Saying it is NOT involved is False. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Efferent arteriolar tone
statements.explanation: Efferent arteriolar tone is highly involved; constricting it increases glomerular pressure and GFR. Therefore, saying it is not involved is False. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Afferent arteriolar tone
statements.explanation: Afferent arteriolar resistance is the main site of GFR autoregulation. Saying it is not involved is False. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Na+ content in the distal tubule
statements.explanation: The macula densa senses sodium content in the distal tubule to regulate GFR via tubuloglomerular feedback. Saying it is not involved is False. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Juxtaglomerular apparatus
statements.explanation: The JGA is the essential structural complex responsible for autoregulating GFR. Saying it is not involved is False. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following constitutes Juxtaglomerular apparatus

statements.text: Macula Densa cells
statements.explanation: The macula densa is a specialized plaque of cells in the distal tubule that forms a crucial sensory component of the juxtaglomerular apparatus. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Juxta-glomerular cells
statements.explanation: These specialized smooth muscle cells in the afferent arteriole synthesize and secrete renin, forming the effector arm of the apparatus. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Glomerular mesangial cells
statements.explanation: Extraglomerular mesangial cells (Lacis cells) sit between the macula densa and arterioles, acting as a communication link within the apparatus. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Afferent arteriole
statements.explanation: The afferent arteriole is structurally part of the apparatus, as it houses the granular juxtaglomerular cells. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Efferent arteriole
statements.explanation: The efferent arteriole is also intimately connected at the vascular pole and interacts with the signals generated by the apparatus. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Regarding sodium potassium pump

statements.text: It Pumps 2 Na+ into the cell and 3 K+ out of the cell
statements.explanation: The pump works in the exact opposite ratio: it moves 3 Na+ OUT of the cell and 2 K+ INTO the cell against their gradients. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: It is important in secondary active transport
statements.explanation: By maintaining a low intracellular sodium concentration, the pump creates the steep gradient that drives almost all secondary active transport processes. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: It is located on luminal side of the proximal convoluted tubule
statements.explanation: In renal tubular cells, the Na+/K+ ATPase pumps are strictly located on the basolateral membrane (blood side), not the luminal side. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: It is important in the reabsorption of glucose in the proximal convoluted tubule
statements.explanation: The pump creates the sodium gradient required for the SGLT transporters to pull glucose from the lumen into the cell via secondary active transport. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: it requires ATP
statements.explanation: As a primary active transporter moving ions against steep electrochemical gradients, the pump directly consumes ATP to function. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following are endocrine functions of the kidney:

statements.text: Aldosterone production
statements.explanation: Aldosterone is produced by the adrenal cortex (zona glomerulosa) which sits atop the kidney, but it is not produced by the kidney itself. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Erythropoietin production
statements.explanation: The kidneys are the primary endocrine source of erythropoietin, which stimulates red blood cell production in response to hypoxia. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Thrombopoietin production
statements.explanation: Although the kidney produces trace amounts, thrombopoietin is primarily produced by the liver. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Renin hormone secretion
statements.explanation: The kidneys secrete renin, an enzyme that acts as part of an endocrine cascade (RAAS) to regulate blood pressure and volume. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Vitamin D3 activation
statements.explanation: The kidneys perform the final hydroxylation step (using 1-alpha-hydroxylase) to convert inactive vitamin D into active calcitriol. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

questions.stem: A person with reduced urine PH the following could be the reason

statements.text: Damage to the principle cell
statements.explanation: Principal cells handle sodium and potassium transport. Acid-base regulation (which determines urine pH) is handled by the intercalated cells. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Damage to the intercalated cells type A
statements.explanation: Type A cells secrete acid. If they are damaged, the kidneys cannot excrete acid, which would lead to a higher (more alkaline) urine pH, not reduced. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Damage to the intercalated cells type B
statements.explanation: Type B cells secrete bicarbonate into urine. If damaged, less bicarbonate buffers the urine, leading to an excess of H+ and a reduced (acidic) urine pH. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Dysfunctional proximal convoluted tubule
statements.explanation: Dysfunction in the proximal tubule impairs bicarbonate reabsorption, initially causing a flood of bicarbonate into the urine, raising the pH. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Diabetic keto acidosis (DKA)
statements.explanation: In DKA, massive amounts of ketoacids enter the blood. The kidneys compensate by heavily secreting hydrogen ions, resulting in a profoundly reduced urine pH. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Regarding potassium

statements.text: Low PH causes hypokalemia
statements.explanation: A low blood pH (acidosis) causes hydrogen ions to enter cells and potassium to exit, typically resulting in hyperkalemia, not hypokalemia. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Insulin causes hypokalemia
statements.explanation: Insulin strongly stimulates the Na+/K+ ATPase pump, driving potassium from the blood into cells, which can rapidly cause hypokalemia. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Aldosterone decreases its secretion
statements.explanation: Aldosterone acts on principal cells to stimulate sodium reabsorption and explicitly increases the active secretion of potassium into the urine. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Secreted by principle cell at the DCT
statements.explanation: The principal cells in the late distal convoluted tubule and cortical collecting duct are the primary sites for actively secreting potassium into the tubular fluid. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Is reabsorbed by intercalated cells at the proximal convoluted tubule
statements.explanation: Potassium reabsorption in the proximal tubule is paracellular and passive. Alpha-intercalated cells reabsorb potassium only later in the collecting ducts during hypokalemia. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Diluting mechanism of the kidney:

statements.text: Takes place during water excess in the body
statements.explanation: When a person drinks excess water, the kidneys engage the diluting mechanism to excrete the surplus water without losing essential salts. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Is associated with production of high volume, less concentrated urine
statements.explanation: The primary outcome of the diluting mechanism is a large volume of urine that has an osmolarity significantly lower than that of blood plasma. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Is associated with secretion of Antidiuretic hormone by the hypothalamus
statements.explanation: To produce dilute urine, the secretion of ADH must be strongly suppressed, not stimulated. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Is associated with increased permeability of collecting duct to urea and water
statements.explanation: In the absence of ADH (during dilution), the collecting ducts become highly impermeable to both water and urea, trapping water in the urine. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Is the principle biological mechanism by which kidneys help us to retain more water
statements.explanation: The diluting mechanism sheds excess water. It is the concentrating mechanism (via ADH) that helps the body retain and conserve water during dehydration. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: During counter-current multiplier mechanism:

statements.text: Water reabsorption at the thin descending limb is via aquaporin-1 (AQP1)
statements.explanation: The thin descending limb is highly permeable to water due to the constitutive presence of Aquaporin-1 channels. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Water reabsorption takes place at thick ascending
statements.explanation: The thick ascending limb is structurally impermeable to water, an essential feature that allows it to dilute the tubular fluid and multiply the medullary gradient. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Urea re-absorption at thin ascending limb is via UT-A3 transporters
statements.explanation: Urea is actively secreted into the thin ascending limb via UT-A2 transporters, while UT-A1/A3 handle reabsorption much later in the inner medullary collecting duct. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: At thin descending limb, salt reabsorption takes place via NKCC secondary active transporters
statements.explanation: NKCC transporters are located exclusively in the thick ascending limb, not the thin descending limb (which handles water, not active salt pumping). (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Both Salt and urea can be re-absorbed at thin ascending limb
statements.explanation: In the thin ascending limb, sodium chloride is passively reabsorbed out, but urea diffuses IN (secretion), so they are not both reabsorbed. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following factors tend to stimulate Renin secretion by juxtaglomerular cells:

statements.text: Parasympathetic signals
statements.explanation: Renin secretion is not controlled by the parasympathetic system; it is the sympathetic system that stimulates its release. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Sympathetic signals
statements.explanation: Activation of sympathetic nerves directly stimulates beta-1 adrenergic receptors on juxtaglomerular cells, triggering renin release. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Prostaglandin release
statements.explanation: Local release of prostaglandins (like PGE2) by the macula densa acts on juxtaglomerular cells to powerfully stimulate renin secretion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Adenosine release
statements.explanation: Unlike most other tissues, adenosine released by the macula densa during high flow binds to A1 receptors and inhibits renin release. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Local stretch receptor
statements.explanation: Increased stretch (due to high blood pressure) actually inhibits renin release. A decrease in stretch is what stimulates it. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following are the roles of kidney when PH falls below normal

statements.text: Secretion of HCO3
statements.explanation: When blood pH is low (acidosis), the kidneys need to conserve base, so they completely halt bicarbonate secretion and maximize its reabsorption. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Formation of new HCO3
statements.explanation: To combat acidosis, tubular cells synthesize new bicarbonate ions while excreting hydrogen ions buffered by titratable acids and ammonium. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Reabsorption of all filtered HCO3
statements.explanation: The very first renal response to acidosis is to completely reabsorb 100% of the bicarbonate filtered into the tubules to prevent any base loss. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Secretion of H+ by principal cells
statements.explanation: Principal cells handle sodium/potassium. Hydrogen ion secretion is specifically handled by the specialized Type A intercalated cells. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Reabsorption of H+ by intercalated cells
statements.explanation: In acidosis, the kidneys need to get rid of acid. Therefore, Type A intercalated cells actively secrete H+ into the urine, not reabsorb it. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following might lead to hyperkalemia (high level of potassium in the blood):

statements.text: Metabolic acidosis
statements.explanation: In metabolic acidosis, excess hydrogen ions enter cells to be buffered, displacing intracellular potassium out into the blood and causing hyperkalemia. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Vomiting and diarrhea
statements.explanation: Severe vomiting and diarrhea result in massive losses of potassium from the gastrointestinal tract, leading to hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: High insulin dose/secretion
statements.explanation: Insulin drives potassium from the blood into cells. High doses of insulin will therefore drop blood potassium levels (hypokalemia). (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Food highly rich in potassium
statements.explanation: While healthy kidneys usually adapt, an acute massive intake of potassium-rich food can cause transient hyperkalemia, especially if renal function is compromised. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Kidney failure/ kidney disease
statements.explanation: The kidneys are the sole route for excreting daily potassium loads. If they fail, potassium accumulates rapidly, resulting in lethal hyperkalemia. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: During breathing

statements.text: Pleural pressure becomes more negative during expiration
statements.explanation: During expiration, the chest cavity shrinks, compressing the lungs and causing the pleural pressure to become less negative (closer to zero). (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Transpulmonary pressure is increased during inspiration
statements.explanation: As pleural pressure drops during inspiration, the pressure difference across the lung wall (transpulmonary pressure) increases, pulling the lungs open. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Alveolar pressure becomes positive during expiration
statements.explanation: The elastic recoil of the lungs compresses the alveolar gas, raising its pressure above atmospheric (+1 cmH2O) to drive air out. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Pleural pressure becomes more negative during inspiration
statements.explanation: As the chest wall expands, the fluid-filled pleural space is stretched, causing the pressure to drop from roughly -5 cmH2O to -7.5 cmH2O. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Diaphragm relaxes during inspiration
statements.explanation: Inspiration is an active process that begins specifically when the diaphragm contracts and flattens to enlarge the chest cavity. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Regarding dead space

statements.text: Alveolar dead space is zero in well ventilated and perfused alveoli
statements.explanation: If an alveolus receives perfect blood flow and ventilation, all its air participates in gas exchange, meaning it contributes exactly zero to dead space. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: If the value is getting higher indicates poor gaseous exchange
statements.explanation: An increasing physiological dead space means more inspired air is reaching zones without blood flow, which is a hallmark of failing, inefficient gas exchange. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: In a 70kg man is about 150mls
statements.explanation: The standard anatomical dead space (the volume of the conducting airways) in an average 70kg adult is approximately 150 ml. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Anatomical dead space is synonymous to conducting zone of the respiratory system
statements.explanation: By definition, the anatomical dead space consists precisely of the conducting zone structures (trachea, bronchi) where walls are too thick for gas exchange. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: It is important when calculating a minute ventilation
statements.explanation: Dead space is irrelevant for calculating total Minute Ventilation (Respiratory Rate x Tidal Volume). It is, however, critical for calculating Alveolar Ventilation. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: In control of breathing

statements.text: Pneumotaxic center controls the duration of filling phase of the lung cycle
statements.explanation: The pneumotaxic center functions to switch off the inspiratory ramp signal, effectively limiting the duration of inspiration (filling phase). (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: CO2 is the potent stimulator of peripheral chemoreceptors
statements.explanation: CO2 acts primarily and potently on the CENTRAL chemoreceptors in the brain. Decreased oxygen (hypoxia) is the primary stimulator of the peripheral chemoreceptors. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Increased concentration of 02 stimulate peripheral chemoreceptors
statements.explanation: Peripheral chemoreceptors fire rapidly in response to DECREASED oxygen (hypoxia), not increased oxygen. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Ventral respiratory group are usually active during normal quiet respiration
statements.explanation: The dorsal respiratory group handles quiet inspiration. The ventral group remains totally inactive during quiet breathing and only activates when high ventilation (forced expiration) is needed. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: A decrease in 02 concentration will cause a decrease in respiratory rate
statements.explanation: A drop in O2 concentration triggers the peripheral chemoreceptors, which forcefully stimulate the respiratory centers to INCREASE the rate and depth of breathing. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following physiological changes occurs when person go to high altitude

statements.text: Oxygen hemoglobin dissociation curve shift to the right
statements.explanation: Hypoxia at high altitude triggers red blood cells to produce more 2,3-DPG, which shifts the curve right to facilitate easier oxygen offloading at the tissues. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Increased pH
statements.explanation: The low oxygen drives hyperventilation, which blows off excessive CO2. This reduces blood carbonic acid, raising the pH (respiratory alkalosis). (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Formation of new blood vessels at the tissue level
statements.explanation: Chronic acclimatization to high altitude involves tissue hypoxia stimulating angiogenesis (new capillary formation) to improve oxygen delivery distance. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Decreased erythropoiesis
statements.explanation: Hypoxia is the strongest stimulant for erythropoietin release from the kidneys, resulting in massively INCREASED red blood cell production. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: immediate increase in cardiac output
statements.explanation: Upon acute exposure to high altitude, heart rate and cardiac output increase immediately to maintain tissue oxygen delivery before blood changes can occur. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Regarding lung volumes

statements.text: Residual volume is the amount of air in the lungs after forceful inspiration
statements.explanation: Residual volume is defined as the volume of air left trapped in the lungs after a maximum, forceful EXPIRATION, not inspiration. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: IRV is extra volume of air inhaled after normal tidal volume inspiration
statements.explanation: Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) represents the maximum additional air you can consciously draw into the lungs above a normal resting breath. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Vital capacity is equal to total lung volume minus residual volume
statements.explanation: Total lung capacity is the sum of vital capacity and residual volume. Rearranging the equation: Vital Capacity = Total Lung Capacity - Residual Volume. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Tidal volume is usually higher in males than in female
statements.explanation: Because lung volumes correlate directly with body size and chest cavity dimensions, all absolute lung volumes (including tidal volume) are statistically larger in adult males. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Functional residual capacity is the amount of air remained in the lungs after normal tidal expiration
statements.explanation: FRC represents the equilibrium point of the respiratory system; it is the resting volume of air remaining when inward lung recoil matches outward chest wall recoil at the end of a quiet exhalation. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following shifts oxygen Hb dissociation curve to the right

statements.text: Low temperature
statements.explanation: Cold temperatures stabilize the bond between oxygen and hemoglobin, causing a shift to the LEFT. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: High pH
statements.explanation: A high pH (alkalosis) means fewer hydrogen ions are present to displace oxygen, causing hemoglobin to hold on tighter, shifting the curve to the LEFT. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Increased 2,3 DPG
statements.explanation: 2,3-DPG binds directly to the hemoglobin beta chains, lowering its affinity for oxygen and forcing a shift to the RIGHT to enhance tissue delivery. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Increased arteriole PO2
statements.explanation: Changes in partial pressure of oxygen (PO2) simply represent movement UP OR DOWN along the existing curve, they do not physically shift the curve itself left or right. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: increased H+ concentration
statements.explanation: High hydrogen ion concentration (low pH) structurally alters hemoglobin, lowering its oxygen affinity (the Bohr effect) and shifting the curve to the RIGHT. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: Which of the following does NOT affect diffusion

statements.text: Surface area for diffusion
statements.explanation: According to Fick's Law, diffusion is directly proportional to the available surface area; therefore it DOES affect diffusion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Concentration gradient
statements.explanation: The pressure/concentration gradient is the primary driving force in Fick's Law, so it heavily affects diffusion. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Solubility of the gas
statements.explanation: Solubility determines the diffusion coefficient; highly soluble gases like CO2 diffuse much faster. Therefore it affects diffusion. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Molecular weight of the gas
statements.explanation: Graham's Law states diffusion is inversely proportional to the square root of molecular weight, so heavier molecules diffuse slower. It affects diffusion. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Breathing frequency
statements.explanation: Breathing frequency changes alveolar ventilation, but it is not a variable in Fick's law of diffusion capacity across the membrane itself. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following statements are true regarding surfactant EXCEPT:

statements.text: Produced by type 2 pneumocytes
statements.explanation: Surfactant is exclusively manufactured and secreted into the alveoli by the cuboidal Type II alveolar epithelial cells. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Reduce surface tension of the alveoli
statements.explanation: The primary biophysical role of the phospholipids in surfactant is to interrupt water-water bonds, significantly reducing surface tension. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Promote stability of the alveoli
statements.explanation: Because its density changes with alveolar radius, surfactant prevents small alveoli from collapsing into larger ones, providing essential stability. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Prevent lung collapse
statements.explanation: Without surfactant, the inward pull of surface tension would overwhelm the lung's structural integrity, causing massive collapse (atelectasis). (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Large alveoli have lower surface tension than small alveoli
statements.explanation: As an alveolus expands, the surfactant molecules spread further apart, actually causing the surface tension in large alveoli to be HIGHER than in small ones. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: The following pairs are collect EXCEPT:

statements.text: Tidal volume = 500 mls
statements.explanation: In a healthy, resting adult, the average volume of air moved per breath (tidal volume) is universally accepted as roughly 500 ml. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Alveolar ventilation = 7500 mls
statements.explanation: Alveolar ventilation is typically (500-150)ml x 12 breaths = 4200 ml/min. 7500 mls is far too high for resting alveolar ventilation. (Source: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 26th edition)

statements.text: Functional residual capacity = volume of gas in the lungs after normal expiration
statements.explanation: This is the exact physiological definition of Functional Residual Capacity (FRC). (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Pulmonary blood flow = 5000 mls/min
statements.explanation: Because the entire cardiac output passes through the lungs, normal resting pulmonary blood flow equals normal cardiac output, roughly 5000 ml/min. (Source: Human Physiology, the Mechanisms of body Function; by Vander, 6th edition)

statements.text: Physiological dead space = 150 mls
statements.explanation: In a healthy person with no alveolar dead space, the physiological dead space perfectly matches the anatomical dead space of 150 mls. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

questions.stem: With respect to regional gas exchange in the up right lung

statements.text: Ventilation is greater at the top of the lungs
statements.explanation: Because gravity compresses the bases more at rest, the base of the lung actually expands more and receives significantly greater ventilation than the apex. (Source: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 15th edition)

statements.text: Perfusion is much greater at the top of the lungs compared with the bases
